questions.text: Who started the Asiatic Society of Bengal in 1784?
questions.explanation: William Jones, a linguist, arrived in Calcutta and started the Asiatic Society to study Indian history and culture.

questions.text: A person who can read, write and teach several languages is known as a:
questions.explanation: A linguist is someone who knows and studies several languages.

questions.text: The scholars who have a deep knowledge of the language and culture of Asia are called:
questions.explanation: Orientalists believed in studying Indian classical texts to understand the heritage of the East.

questions.text: In which year was a Madrasa set up in Calcutta to promote the study of Arabic, Persian and Islamic law?
questions.explanation: The Calcutta Madrasa was established in 1781 by Warren Hastings.

questions.text: The Hindu College was established in ________ in 1791 to encourage the study of ancient Sanskrit texts.
questions.explanation: The Benaras Hindu College was established to train administrators for the Company.

questions.text: Who famously attacked the Orientalists and saw India as an uncivilised country that needed to be civilised?
questions.explanation: Macaulay believed that 'a single shelf of a good European library was worth the whole native literature of India and Arabia'.

questions.text: Which Act made English the medium of instruction for higher education in India?
questions.explanation: Based on Macaulay's Minute, the 1835 Act was passed to promote Western learning.

questions.text: What was the official title of 'Wood's Despatch' of 1854?
questions.explanation: Issued by Charles Wood, it outlined the educational policy for India.

questions.text: According to Wood's Despatch, European learning would improve the ________ character of Indians.
questions.explanation: The British believed European education would make Indians truthful and honest, thus providing reliable civil servants.

questions.text: Universities were established in Calcutta, Bombay and Madras in ________.
questions.explanation: During the revolt of 1857, the British government was busy setting up universities as per Wood's Despatch.

questions.text: William Adam was a Scottish missionary who toured the districts of Bengal and Bihar to report on the progress of:
questions.explanation: He found over 1 lakh pathshalas with a flexible system of education.

questions.text: In the flexible system of pathshalas, when were classes not held?
questions.explanation: Classes were suspended during harvest so that children from rural families could work in the fields.

questions.text: After 1854, the Company appointed ________ to improve the system of vernacular education.
questions.explanation: Each Pandit was in charge of 4-5 schools to standardise teaching and introduce regular time-tables.

questions.text: Who argued that 'English education has enslaved us'?
questions.explanation: Gandhi believed Western education created a sense of inferiority in the minds of Indians.

questions.text: According to Gandhi, education should develop a person's ________.
questions.explanation: He emphasized that literacy alone was not education; one must work with hands and learn a craft.

questions.text: Who established Santiniketan in 1901?
questions.explanation: Tagore started the school in a rural setting to allow creative learning in harmony with nature.

questions.text: Tagore's Santiniketan was known as the 'Abode of ________'.
questions.explanation: Santiniketan literally means an abode of peace.

questions.text: Which of these was a point of difference between Gandhi and Tagore regarding education?
questions.explanation: While Gandhi was critical of modern machinery, Tagore wanted to combine Indian tradition with Western science and technology.

questions.text: The term 'Munshi' refers to a person who can:
questions.explanation: Munshis were often employed by the British to learn local administrative languages.

questions.text: Orientalists believed that the British should respect Indian ________.
questions.explanation: They felt that only by studying the classical past could the British win the hearts of the 'natives'.

questions.text: Thomas Macaulay believed that the ________ language would allow Indians to read the finest literature in the world.
questions.explanation: He viewed English as a gateway to Western science and philosophy.

questions.text: The 1854 Despatch emphasized the ________ benefits of European learning.
questions.explanation: It argued that European education would help Indians recognise the advantages that flow from the expansion of trade.

questions.text: What was the name of the missionary who established the Serampore Mission?
questions.explanation: William Carey was a Baptist missionary associated with the Serampore Mission in Bengal.

questions.text: Before 1854, pathshalas were often held under a ________.
questions.explanation: Pathshalas had no fixed building and were often held under trees or in the guru's home.

questions.text: The Serampore Mission was established on the banks of which river?
questions.explanation: It was situated near Calcutta, on the Hooghly river.

questions.text: Who among the following was a linguist?
questions.explanation: William Jones knew Greek, Latin, French, English, Hebrew, Arabic, and Persian.

questions.text: The 'Anglicists' favored ________ over Oriental learning.
questions.explanation: Anglicists believed in the superiority of Western knowledge and science.

questions.text: Pathshalas that followed the new rules of the Company after 1854 received:
questions.explanation: Schools that agreed to the new system were supported by government money.

questions.text: Mahatma Gandhi wanted to bring ________ back to Indian education.
questions.explanation: He wanted education to help Indians recover their pride in their own culture.

questions.text: Rabindranath Tagore believed that children should learn in a ________ environment.
questions.explanation: Tagore felt that childhood should be a time of self-learning, away from the rigid 'prison' of British schools.

questions.text: The term 'Vernacular' refers to:
questions.explanation: In colonial India, it was used to distinguish local languages from English.

questions.text: Which city was NOT a site for the setting up of universities in 1857?
questions.explanation: Universities were set up in the three Presidency towns: Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay.

questions.text: William Jones and Henry Thomas Colebrooke were both ________.
questions.explanation: They both shared a deep respect for ancient cultures and Indian heritage.

questions.text: Mahatma Gandhi criticized the British for prioritizing ________ over oral knowledge.
questions.explanation: He believed that focusing only on textbooks ignored the practical experience of life.

questions.text: The Asiatic Researches was a journal published by:
questions.explanation: It was established by William Jones to publish research on Indian history and science.

questions.text: What happened to gurus whose pathshalas did not follow Company rules?
options: They were given no government support
questions.explanation: Independent gurus found it difficult to compete with government-aided schools.

questions.text: Wood’s Despatch argued that a ________ of educated Indians would be created by Western education.
questions.explanation: The British hoped that Western education would create a class of people loyal to British rule.

questions.text: Which year is associated with the English Education Act?
questions.explanation: 1835 was the year Macaulay's Minute was implemented.

questions.text: The term 'Madrasa' is an Arabic word for a place of ________.
questions.explanation: It refers to any type of school, college or university.

questions.text: Who among these supported Oriental learning?
questions.explanation: Warren Hastings was an early supporter of studying Indian traditions and languages.
